coredata.dc:title: Study on energy-saving techniques of the lithium-ion batteries cooling system using a backup battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Kang
Lou, Yi-Long
Feng, Xiao-Hui
Li, Zhen-Zhe
Zhang, Mei-Ling
coredata.dc:description: The power battery in an electric vehicle is an essential part, and the damage to the battery will have a substantial impact on its thermal characteristics. In order to improve the reliability of the air-cooled lithium-ion battery packs in the high temperature environments, this paper offers a more useful and general optimization strategy for the design of the thermal management system for the batteries which have a damaged battery. To ensure the optimal heat dissipation and prolong the battery string operation, the damaged batteries should be swapped out with the backup batteries. The relationship between the target and the design variables which was established using the quadratic polynomial response function is examined. The experimental cases are selected using the optimal Latin hypercube design approach. After optimizing through the multi-island genetic approach, the error between the simulation results and the prediction results is only 0.06 %. The maximum temperature and the maximum temperature difference of the ideal structure are reduced by 4.58 % and 28.05 %, respectively. Then, the backup battery is selected as the sole battery that has the highest temperature of the ideal structure, and each broken battery is replaced by the backup battery when the damaged position is determined. Finally, a parametric equation connecting the temperature and the flow rate is created, and the inlet flow rate is tuned to match the optimal heat dissipation state of the battery pack. The results of the battery thermal management system having a damaged battery in various situations have an important significance for adjusting the operating conditions of the cooling system for the batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Power battery
Backup battery
Optimal Latin hypercube
Response Function
Multi-island genetic method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Comparison of single battery discharging with experimental data
Choice of backup battery and replacement of damaged battery

Also, this study is funded by Patent Navigation Project of Wenzhou University of China.

Results of the backup on and off.
Coefficients of quadratic function.
Sample results after changing inlet velocity.

head.ce:title: Study on energy-saving techniques of the lithium-ion batteries cooling system using a backup battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Wenzhou University, Zhejiang, 325035, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, Wenzhou University, Zhejiang, 325035, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Wenzhou Medical University, Zhejiang, 325035, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Wenzhou Medical University, Zhejiang, 325035, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The power battery in an electric vehicle is an essential part, and the damage to the battery will have a substantial impact on its thermal characteristics. In order to improve the reliability of the air-cooled lithium-ion battery packs in the high temperature environments, this paper offers a more useful and general optimization strategy for the design of the thermal management system for the batteries which have a damaged battery. To ensure the optimal heat dissipation and prolong the battery string operation, the damaged batteries should be swapped out with the backup batteries. The relationship between the target and the design variables which was established using the quadratic polynomial response function is examined. The experimental cases are selected using the optimal Latin hypercube design approach. After optimizing through the multi-island genetic approach, the error between the simulation results and the prediction results is only 0.06 %. The maximum temperature and the maximum temperature difference of the ideal structure are reduced by 4.58 % and 28.05 %, respectively. Then, the backup battery is selected as the sole battery that has the highest temperature of the ideal structure, and each broken battery is replaced by the backup battery when the damaged position is determined. Finally, a parametric equation connecting the temperature and the flow rate is created, and the inlet flow rate is tuned to match the optimal heat dissipation state of the battery pack. The results of the battery thermal management system having a damaged battery in various situations have an important significance for adjusting the operating conditions of the cooling system for the batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para:
specific heat,
J ∙
(
k g ∙ K
)
− 1

heat source of battery, W
∙
m
− 3

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, with the intensification of the environmental pollution and the shortage of the oil resources, the electric vehicles using clean energy have slowly entered the historical stage [1,2]. As the energy storage component of the electric vehicles, the cost of the batteries has a significant effect on the total cost of the electric vehicles [3]. There are four types of the batteries: the lead-acid batteries, the nickel-metal hydride batteries, the supercapacitors and the lithium-ion batteries. Compared with most other batteries, the lithium-ion batteries have the advantages such as light weight, high energy density, recyclability, long lifespan, and high specific power [4,5], making them the most suitable battery as the power source for the electric vehicles. However, during the rapid charging and discharging processes of lithium-ion batteries, a large amount of heat is released through a series of chemical reactions [6], causing the internal temperature of the lithium-ion battery to rise. The lithium-ion batteries are susceptible to the temperature caused by their charging and discharging rate [7]. Therefore, it is necessary to introduce a lithium-ion battery thermal management system (BTMS) to control the internal temperature of the battery. According to the optimal operating temperature requirements of the lithium-ion batteries, it is necessary to ensure that the operating temperature of the lithium-ion battery is controlled between 293.15 K and 313.15 K when designing a battery thermal management system [8,9]. The maximum temperature difference (TD) between the batteries within the battery pack is controlled within 5 K [10,11]. Once the working temperature of a lithium-ion battery is too high, it can easily cause irreversible damage to the battery, and it can also lead to accidents such as spontaneous combustion in the electric vehicles in severe cases [12,13]. Therefore, efficiently designing and optimizing a BTMS to control the temperature and the temperature difference of the battery pack within the optimal operating temperature range is a key issue that needs to be urgently addressed.

The air cooling, the liquid cooling, and the phase-change cooling are the three most often utilized types in BTMS today [14]. Because of its lightweight design, inexpensive production, and maintenance, the air cooling is an often utilized technology [15]. Many researchers have studied it due to its exceptional benefits.

Saechan et al. [16] designed a heat dissipation method with a heat sink, and studied the effects of the inlet speed, the discharge rate, and the battery arrangement structure on the cooling performance, and had a very significant effect on reducing the maximum temperature of the battery pack and the uniformity of the battery. Gao et al. [17] proposed a finned air cooling method, which increased the structural optimization and improved the heat dissipation effect compared with traditional air cooling. Wang et al. [18] installed a spoiler between the cells to reduce the volume and the maximum temperature of the battery pack. Zhang et al. [19] added a secondary air outlet and a spoiler to the traditional Z-channel heat dissipation system and discussed the influence of the shape, the length, and the number of the spoiler on the heat dissipation of the battery pack. The optimized structure improved the heat dissipation performance and saved the cost. Zhang et al. [20] designed a dual-outlet T-channel heat dissipation system based on the basic Z-channel heat dissipation system. The effects of the battery layout, top angle, and the inlet and the outlet structural parameters on the cooling performance of the battery pack were discussed, and the optimal structure was obtained by orthogonal analysis. Both the maximum temperature and the maximum temperature difference have dropped. Chen et al. [21] changed the inlet and outlet positions and studied the influence of the above design on the cooling performance of the battery system by combining the numerical simulation and the experiment. The results show that the heat dissipation is optimal when the inlet and the outlet of the battery pack are located in the middle of the battery model box. The uniformity of the battery pack is improved and the power consumption is reduced after optimization. Luo et al. [22] developed an X-type dual inlet and outlet symmetrical air-cooled battery thermal management system. Through orthogonal analysis, the entrance position, the exit position, the entrance angle, and the exit angle are optimized. After optimization, the maximum temperature and the maximum temperature difference are decreased respectively.

The number of spoilers and the fins is increased in the aforementioned research to improve cooling performance. In addition, the inlet and the outlet are studied, the heat dissipation structure is optimized using orthogonal analysis, and the battery thermal management system's energy efficiency is enhanced. The objective of this work is to improve the spatial organization of the battery pack by continuous sample optimization for reducing the maximum temperature difference and increasing the energy savings of the battery. Lastly, a backup battery can be installed in place of a single damaged battery. The battery pack is then brought to the ideal operating temperature by varying the flow rate of the inlet.

In a word, the structural continuous optimization and the numerical analysis of a lithium-ion battery pack with a backup battery are presented in this work to solve the problem that the battery pack has a single damaged battery.
In this paper, the effects of having a backup unit on a conventional air-cooled lithium-ion battery pack were investigated in the high temperature environment.

Solidworks software was used in this work to create a battery pack model. The 18650 lithium-ion battery with a rated capacity of 3.4Ah and a nominal voltage of 3.7V was chosen as the investigation battery. The battery cooling system has the dimensions of 120mm × 70mm × 85 mm. As indicated in Fig. 1 , there are 10 lithium-ion batteries were distributed in the cooling system as the equal intervals of 4 mm. The cells in the first row were designated as 1, 3, 5, 7 and 9, and the cells in the second row were marked as 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10. Both the inlet and the outlet dimensions are 100 mm by 8 mm.

The actual analysis of the battery heat transfer process is very complicated. For ease of calculation, the following four assumptions are made about the system.
(a)
Each cell is a uniform body heat source.
(b)
The temperature changes do not affect the physical parameters of the battery material.
(c)
It is considered that the inertial stress caused by the fluid itself is zero and there is no slip at the fluid-structure coupling boundary.
(d)
The air temperature and the battery surface temperature are low and uniform, ignoring the radiation heat transfer of the battery.

The heat conduction of the battery, the heat conduction of the air, and the coupled convection heat transfer of fluid domain between the surface of the battery and the air as well as between the air and the surface of the cooling box are all factors in the simulation of air-cooled heat dissipation of lithium-ion battery packs. The heat conduction in the battery can be described as Eq. (1). The discharge simulation of the single battery will use the unsteady model, and the steady model is for the cooling performance simulation of the battery pack. Also, the governing equations including the continuity equation, the momentum equation, and the energy equation shown in Eqs. (2)–(6) will be used for the fluid domain simulation which is described in the steady model form [23].
(1)
∂
(
ρ T
)
∂ τ

)

S H
Where
τ
is the time, T represents the temperature,
k

c P

ρ

S H
is the heat source of the battery.

The simulation model's material attributes are displayed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of single battery discharging with experimental data
ce:section.ce:para:
Zhu [24] designed a semi-enclosed miniature liquid cooling pipe heat dissipation structure with a cylindrical battery structure. Through structural optimization of the inlet position and the battery arrangement, the maximum temperature and the temperature uniformity of the battery thermal management system were significantly decreased. Based on the previous work of the other researcher, Ansys-fluent was used in this study to simulate a single battery for investigating the thermal characteristics of the lithium-ion battery. The governing equation for the thermal simulation of the single battery was shown in Eq. (1). In this study, the battery is heated at a rate of 18439.14 W/m3. In the other word, the heat source was distributed uniformly in the total volume of the battery which means that each surface of the battery is heated too. Also, the convective heat transfer was applied for the surface of the battery, and the coefficient of the convective heat transfer was 8 W/(m2
∙
K). Also, the temperature of the environment is 298.15 K for comparing with the experimental data.

The temperature contour diagram of a single battery's central circular cross-section is likewise displayed in Fig. 2 , and it is clear that the battery's maximum temperature rises as the battery discharge rate increases. This is due to the fact that the battery's internal heat production rate progressively increases when a single battery's discharge rate increases. Because the convective heat transfer has a very limited heat dissipation impact, heat builds up inside the battery and the temperature of the battery center increases significantly. Fig. 3 shows the comparison between the simulation of the single battery and the experimental data at three different discharge rates. The maximum error is 0.72 %. The findings demonstrate that the heat production model can accurately capture the lithium-ion battery heat generation.

For simulating the battery thermal management system, the governing equations including Eq. (1) for the batteries and Eqs. (2)–(6) for the cooling air were used. The finite volume method is used for this steady state simulation. Also, a second-order upwind strategy and a coupled algorithm were used in the simulation method. The fluid flow is turbulent because the Reynolds number is 4900. Therefore, the standard k-epsilon turbulence model is used for heat convection [22]. The standard k-epsilon model is a model to solve the turbulence fields based on the turbulence energy and the dissipation rate, which has good robustness and economy, and has wide applications in engineering.

In this simulation, the heat source of the battery was uniformly distributed in the total volume of the battery as a value of 18439.14 W/m3. The inlet speed is set to 4 m/s, while the outlet uses the outflow condition. It means that the heat transfer can be performed using the convective term shown in the left side of Eq. (6) and the temperature difference between the internal and the outlet of the cooling system. Heat convection is the boundary condition of the surface of the cooling system. It means that the heat transfer is also performed due to environmental temperature. The heat transfer coefficient was set to 8 W/(m2
∙
K), and the air temperature was 308.15 K for considering the high temperature environment condition. The default values were used for the other parameters. The grid split of the battery pack is depicted in Figs. 4 and 5
. The structured grid was used for the solid domain of the battery. To solve the problem of having too many blocks due to complex geometry, the fluid domain used the unstructured grid.
At the same time, to improve the accuracy of the simulation, the contact portion between the battery and the fluid domain was encrypted as illustrated in Fig. 6 .

Fig. 7 illustrates that when there were more than 1250013 grids, the difference in the results for the maximum temperature was less than 0.03 %. Therefore, utilizing 1250013 grids for the simulated decomposition computation will yield the desired results.

In the simulation experiment, a benchmark model was utilized to determine the model's maximum temperature under the beginning circumstances, as shown in Fig. 8 . The battery pack needed to be optimized since the maximum temperature of the battery pack exceeded 313.15 K and the maximum temperature differential exceeded 5 K.

To determine the battery pack's maximum temperature, set x1, x2, x3, and x4 as the independent variables. Accordingly, the variables x1, x2, x3, and x4 represent the battery's gap in the x direction, the battery pack's height above the bottom of the battery box in the y direction, the distance moved in the positive direction of the z axis and the gap in the z direction.

We conducted the simulation by giving each variable a value twice to test its sensitivity to the maximum temperature of the battery pack. We are all aware that the battery's performance was primarily affected by the battery module's peak temperature. As a result, we chose to adjust the battery pack's maximum temperature to the desired level as indicated in Table 2 and Fig. 9 . Comparisons were also done with the initial case (case 0), which was displayed in Table 2.

Fig. 9 demonstrates how different variable elements affect the goal value. The change of the gap in the x direction caused the most visible change in the battery pack's maximum temperature, while the change of the gap in the z direction caused the least change. Therefore, the variables used in the following calculation were x1, x2 and x3.
Table 3 displays the three independent variables' upper and lower boundaries in this study, which were determined using the battery box's structural design.

35 experimental points were chosen using the best latin hypercube sampling technique. The fitting between the variables and the response was more precise and realistic when using the ideal latin hypercube design, which increases the uniformity of the random latin hypercube designs. The ideal latin hypercube design has excellent space filling and balance and distributes all of the test points as evenly as possible inside the design space [25,26].

The target value for this investigation was the battery pack's maximum temperature, and Table 4 displayed the analysis's findings for the chosen experimental points. A minimum of (N+1) × (N+2)/2 samples must be used to create the second-order response surface model, where N is the total number of the test variables. In this article, there were three design variables and a minimum of 10 samples. This work uses ANSYS software to create a simulation experiment using 35 distinctive sample points to create a response surface model with the high fitting accuracy. We perform this work using the design of experiments (DOE) component of the iSIGHT platform. Throughout the process of fitting the response surface, a quadratic full polynomial is created. The response surface model was fully fitted using the least squares method. The battery pack's maximum temperature y was approximated by the response surface model.

The relationship between the objective and the design variables was built using a fitting function represented by Eq (7), where y was the objective function and x was the design variable. The coefficients for the fitting function were derived from the analysis results as shown in Table 5 . As the quality assessment value of the fitting function, R2 in this study was 0.967, which can tell us that the quality of the fitting function was high enough. The error between the prediction model results and the training model is shown in Fig. 10 .

To see the trend links between the input values x1, x2 and x3 as well as the desired value y, the iSIGHT approximation model visualization was used as seen in Fig. 11 . Fig. 12 illustrates the connection between the influence of x1, x2 and x3 on the target at the same time.
(7)
y = a
x 1
2
+ b
x 2
2
+ c
x 3
2
+ d
x 1
x 2
+ e
x 1
x 3
+ f
x 2
x 3
+ g
x 1
+ h
x 2
+ i
x 3
+ j

The declining order of the degree of influence on y was shown in the figure above: x1 > x2 > x3. This was consistent with the preliminary inference in the sensitivity analysis in section 3.1, and the value of y rises as x1 and x2 were decreased and raised, respectively. We should decrease the value of y as much as feasible because the target value is the battery pack's maximum temperature. More significant values for x1 and less significant values for x2 will result in a decrease in the maximum temperature.

The fit function obtained after the continuous design of the battery pack is optimized, the backup battery is determined from the optimal structure, and the import and the export static pressure comparison between the basic model and the optimal model can be obtained to study the energy saving. The process uses a parallel genetic algorithm with grouping as its foundation constructed using the conventional genetic process. In the other words, the multi-island genetic algorithm is a genetic algorithm that competes with traditional genetic algorithms which divide the entire crowd into smaller sections [27]. To effectively increase the calculation speed of the multi-island genetic algorithm of each island, each island undertakes a migration operation over a predetermined period. The genetic diversity of the human population has increased as a result of several sub populations that separately emerged to prevent. The phenomenon of the traditional genetic algorithms from occurring in the entire population prematurely, which is beneficial to locate the global best practice [28,29].
A multi-island genetic algorithm was utilized in this work, and the three design variables' ideal parameters were found.

There were ten sub populations, thirty islands, ten genetic generations, one crossover, and rates of 0.01 for mutation and migration. The ideal solution was discovered after 3000 optimization iterations.
Following optimization, we may draw a figure that illustrates how y is affected by x1, and x2 as seen in Fig. 13 .

As we can see, there were more chilly spots overall. Once more, the expected ideal temperature can be seen in the image's lower right corner. The estimated peak temperature was mainly located. Consequently, the temperature of the battery pack was reduced as much as possible.

In other words, x1 should be as large as feasible, and x2 should be as tiny as possible. The space should be left in the x direction as much as possible, and the space should be left in the y direction from the bottom as little as possible.

The model was solved with the aid of iSIGHT software. x1, x2, and x3 were respectively 5.99 mm, 5.06 mm, and 0.71 mm after optimization. The final y value was 312.318 K. In Fig. 14 , the temperature contour map is displayed. The margin of error was minuscule and insignificant. Fig. 15 illustrates how we compare each cell in the improved battery pack to the initial when the three design variables were 4 mm, 4 mm, and 10 mm, respectively, the initial model demonstrated that the battery pack's maximum temperature was 314.40 K. The battery pack's temperature was reduced by 1.891K, or 4.58 %, indicating that the cooling optimization objective was accomplished.

As shown in Fig. 16 , we drew the contour map of the inlet and the outlet pressure distribution of the initial model and the optimized model and compared the bar chart of the inlet and the outlet static pressure. It can be seen that the static pressure drop value of the optimized structure is smaller than that of the initial structure, which means the energy saving.

In this section, the fitting function obtained from the above part of the samples was used to optimize the structure, and it also provided the basis for the selection of the backup battery in the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Choice of backup battery and replacement of damaged battery

ce:section.ce:para: In this section, we want to select a battery as the backup battery which can be used to replace a single damaged battery.

As shown in Fig. 17 , the optimized single battery with the greatest temperature was chosen as the backup battery, and its heating rate was set to 0W/m3. As indicated in Table 6 , the simulation results with and without the backup batteries were compared to the outcomes of heating and not heating the backup batteries. Since the backup battery does not heat up, the battery pack's maximum temperature decreases by 0.215 K.

The backup battery was then turned on under the presumption that the battery pack was malfunctioning as a result of damage sustained by the operating single battery. We also adjusted the damaged battery's heating rate to 0 W/m3, making a total of nine cases. The backup battery also heated properly at the same time.

Fig. 18 displays the simulation results for the nine groups of settings. We can determine that the maximum temperature in the seven groups of condition was higher than that of the backup battery without heating, and the maximum temperature in the two groups was lower, based on a comparison between the maximum temperature of the battery pack obtained after simulation and the maximum temperature of the backup battery without heating in Fig. 19 . Therefore, when the backup battery was turned on, we needed to change the inlet flow rate of the nine groups and adjust the flow rate to reach the maximum temperature of the battery pack to swiftly perform the thermal management of the battery pack under the typical circumstances, without heating the damaged battery.

The maximum temperature of the battery pack will change depending on whether the inlet flow is increased or decreased. We changed the inlet flow rate three times in total to simulate the maximum temperature of 27 battery packs, as shown in Fig. 20 , in order to make the maximum temperature of the battery pack with the damaged unit after flow adjustment equal to the maximum temperature of the battery pack without the damaged optimization previously.

Three flow rates as an input x, the maximum temperature as an input y, and the function presented in Formula 8 may be calculated to get the coefficients a, b, and c for the battery pack architecture in each scenario.
(8)
y = a
x 2
+ b x + c
The calculation results are shown in Table 7 .

We may create nine equations using the information in Table 7. The flow value we require can be obtained by taking the maximum battery pack temperature that can be reached without heating the damaged cell as input y. The results were displayed in Table 8 after simulating the battery pack's maximum temperature under nine different types of the intake flow. Fig. 21 illustrates the comparison between the backup battery with heating optimization results and the simulation results of the computed values.The largest difference between the computed and simulated values was 0.015K, which was essentially insignificant.

There is no difference between the battery pack's maximum temperature and ideal operating temperature after modifying the flow rate of the aforementioned nine battery damage instances using the fitting function, which is beneficial to the battery pack's stable functioning. Additionally, it offers a feasible plan for the battery heat management system in the event of having a damaged unit.

In this study, we addressed a significant issue when the damaged battery is unable to function correctly and must be replaced by the backup battery. When this occurs, we should take all necessary precautions to keep the battery pack's maximum temperature within the usual operating temperature range to retain the dependability of its performance.

This work included the design of a battery pack model with extra cells. Firstly, the distribution characteristics of the various spatial structures of the battery pack were computed using the optimal Latin hypercube sampling technique, assuming a constant inlet flow. The response surface fitting function and the multi-island genetic algorithm are combined to provide a largely acceptable design point. Then, the optimal operating conditions are reached when the battery is 0.77 mm in the positive direction of the z-axis, the gap in the x direction is 5.99 mm, and the bottom surface of the battery pack is 5.06 mm. According to the results, the revised battery pack structure's maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference are lowered by 4.58 % and 28.05 %, respectively, and lowering the inlet static pressure can save energy. After choosing the backup battery and turning on the flow rate of the inlet, the damaged battery was replaced. A functional link between the maximum temperature of the battery pack and the inlet flow rate was discovered by fitting the quadratic functions to the simulation results of sample points acquired by varying the inlet flow rate. In this way, the ideal operating temperature can be changed right away if a single battery of any design breaks and the backup battery is turned on.

Kang Zhang: Writing – original draft. Yi-Long Lou: Investigation. Xiao-Hui Feng: Methodology, Formal analysis. Zhen-Zhe Li: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Mei-Ling Zhang: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the Wenzhou Science and Technology Bureau of China under Grant No. G2023011, and the research is funded by the Wenzhou Association for Science and Technology of China with Grant No. KJFW09, the Master's Innovation Foundation of Wenzhou University of China with Grant No. 3162024004115. Also, this study is funded by Patent Navigation Project of Wenzhou University of China.
